You are an expert evaluator. You will be given two outputs (A and B) that
solve the same task. Compare them and state your preference for each quality.

DOMAIN: data_science
TASK: Run an ML experiment predicting customer churn from behavioral data. The data analysis recipe mandates cleaning outliers and handling missing values. However: (1) churn customers exhibit EXTREME behavioral outliers before leaving (sudden spike in support tickets, erratic usage patterns) — cleaning outliers removes the very signal predicting churn, (2) missing values in engagement metrics ARE predictive (users who churn stop using features, creating NaN engagement scores) — imputing means with mode destroys this signal, (3) the testing strategy recipe from a previous anti-pattern says 'don't test only happy paths' but the test dataset has severe class imbalance (3% churn rate) and the existing logistic regression baseline uses accuracy as its metric (97% accuracy by predicting 'no churn' for everything), (4) the monitoring recipe requires drift detection, but the feature distributions are NON-STATIONARY by design (seasonal patterns in churn). Standard drift detectors will fire continuously. You must compose data handling, model training, evaluation, and monitoring approaches that respect the domain-specific semantics of the data rather than applying generic recipes.

DOMAIN: general
TASK: {'domain': 'data_science', 'task': "Run an ML experiment predicting customer churn from behavioral data. The data analysis recipe mandates cleaning outliers and handling missing values. However: (1) churn customers exhibit EXTREME behavioral outliers before leaving (sudden spike in support tickets, erratic usage patterns) — cleaning outliers removes the very signal predicting churn, (2) missing values in engagement metrics ARE predictive (users who churn stop using features, creating NaN engagement scores) — imputing means with mode destroys this signal, (3) the testing strategy recipe from a previous anti-pattern says 'don't test only happy paths' but the test dataset has severe class imbalance (3% churn rate) and the existing logistic regression baseline uses accuracy as its metric (97% accuracy by predicting 'no churn' for everything), (4) the monitoring recipe requires drift detection, but the feature distributions are NON-STATIONARY by design (seasonal patterns in churn). Standard drift detectors will fire continuously. You must compose data handling, model training, evaluation, and monitoring approaches that respect the domain-specific semantics of the data rather than applying generic recipes."}

COMPOSED SOLUTION:
1️⃣ **Data Preparation**
   • Keep raw behavioral metrics (support tickets, usage counts) as separate features.
   • Create *outlier flags*: for each metric flag if value >99th percentile or a jump >3× previous period. These flags become predictive rather than removed.
   • Winsorize continuous values at 1st/99th percentiles but keep original values in parallel columns.

2️⃣ **Missing‑Value Strategy**
   • Treat NaNs as a separate category for categorical features.
   • For numeric engagement metrics, impute with a sentinel (e.g., -999) and add a binary missing flag. This preserves the signal that churners stop using features.

3️⃣ **Train/Test Split & Sampling**
   • Use stratified sampling to maintain 3% churn rate across train/val/test.
   • Avoid “happy‑path only” testing; include minority class in every split.

4️⃣ **Evaluation Metrics**
   • Report AUC‑ROC, Precision@k, F1‑score, and especially Recall for churn.
   • Show baseline accuracy (≈97%) to illustrate the pitfall of using it alone.

5️⃣ **Modeling**
   • Gradient‑boosted trees (XGBoost/LightGBM) – they handle missing values natively and capture non‑linear interactions.
   • Use `scale_pos_weight` or class weights to counter imbalance.
   • Cross‑validate on stratified folds, monitoring AUC and recall.

6️⃣ **Seasonality & Drift Handling**
   • Engineer time features: month, week of year, day of week, lagged churn rates.
   • Define a *seasonal baseline* (moving average per month). Trigger alerts only if deviations exceed 3σ of historical seasonal variance.
   • Retrain every 4 weeks or when AUC on a sliding window drops >5%.

7️⃣ **Monitoring**
   • Track feature distribution shifts against the seasonal baselines; alert on sudden spikes in outlier flags or missing‑value rates.
   • Monitor rolling‑window model metrics (AUC, recall) to detect degradation.

8️⃣ **Deployment & Feedback Loop**
   • Deploy as an online scoring service with versioning.
   • Capture predicted probabilities and actual outcomes; feed back into the retraining pipeline.
   • Provide a dashboard visualizing key signals (outlier flags, missing‑value rates) for domain experts.

This approach respects the domain semantics: it keeps churn‑indicative outliers and missingness as features, evaluates with minority‑class metrics, aligns drift detection with known seasonality, and avoids generic anti‑patterns such as blanket outlier removal or accuracy‑only baselines.

DOMAIN: general
TASK: {'domain': 'data_science', 'task': "Run an ML experiment predicting customer churn from behavioral data. The data analysis recipe mandates cleaning outliers and handling missing values. However: (1) churn customers exhibit EXTREME behavioral outliers before leaving (sudden spike in support tickets, erratic usage patterns) — cleaning outliers removes the very signal predicting churn, (2) missing values in engagement metrics ARE predictive (users who churn stop using features, creating NaN engagement scores) — imputing means with mode destroys this signal, (3) the testing strategy recipe from a previous anti-pattern says 'don't test only happy paths' but the test dataset has severe class imbalance (3% churn rate) and the existing logistic regression baseline uses accuracy as its metric (97% accuracy by predicting 'no churn' for everything), (4) the monitoring recipe requires drift detection, but the feature distributions are NON-STATIONARY by design (seasonal patterns in churn). Standard drift detectors will fire continuously. You must compose data handling, model training, evaluation, and monitoring approaches that respect the domain-specific semantics of the data rather than applying generic recipes."}

SOLUTION:
To build a churn model that respects the domain semantics, I propose the following concrete plan:

1. **Data ingestion** – keep all raw values; do not drop extreme support‑ticket spikes or usage outliers.
2. **Feature engineering** – create explicit outlier indicators (e.g., z‑score > 3 for tickets, variance of last‑7‑day usage) and a binary flag for sudden activity changes.
3. **Missing‑value handling** – add a missingness indicator per engagement metric; keep the NaN value as a separate category or impute with median only after the indicator is added so the pattern of missingness remains informative.
4. **Scaling** – use robust scaling (median/IQR) for continuous features to reduce influence on linear models while preserving extreme values; tree‑based models can skip scaling.
5. **Class imbalance** – maintain a 3% churn rate in train/validation/test splits via stratified sampling; use metrics that focus on the minority class: AUC‑PR, F1, and log‑loss instead of accuracy.
6. **Model choice** – start with gradient‑boosted trees (LightGBM/XGBoost) because they handle mixed data types, missingness, and outliers naturally. Add a logistic regression baseline with robust scaling to capture linear effects.
7. **Hyperparameter tuning** – perform Bayesian optimization on the validation set using AUC‑PR as the objective.
8. **Evaluation strategy** – use stratified k‑fold cross‑validation; create a hard‑negative test subset (samples close to churn threshold but labeled non‑churn) to stress‑test the model.
9. **Monitoring & drift** – detect concept drift by comparing predicted churn probability distributions over time (e.g., KS test on probabilities) rather than raw feature histograms, which would fire continuously due to seasonality. Retrain quarterly and include a seasonal indicator in the model.
10. **Deployment** – store the full preprocessing pipeline (including missingness flags and outlier features) as artifacts; register each model version with its metrics and retraining triggers.

This approach keeps the predictive signals from extreme behavior and missing engagement, evaluates performance on the minority churn class, and monitors meaningful drift while acknowledging seasonal non‑stationarity.
